paragraphs.source_preview: Microbenchmarking, tcgen05, 2SM MMA, UMMA, TMA, LDGSTS, UBLKCP, Speed of Light, Distributed Shared Memory, GPC Floorsweeps, SM Yield
By Kimbo Chen and Dylan Patel
Mar 31, 2026 · Paid
Nvidia’s Datacenter Blackwell GPU (SM100) represents one of the largest GPU microarchitecture change in a generation, yet no detailed whitepaper exists. Until t
After our in-depth Nvidia Tensor Core Evolution: From Volta To Blackwell article, SemiAnalysis has spent months of engineering time, tearing into the Blackwell
We open sourced our Blackwell micro-architecture-level benchmarking repo here. Please drop a star if you find it useful.
We thank Nebius and Verda for providing B200 nodes for microbenchmarking. Their B200 nodes have the correct hardware counters enabled that makes NCU profiling p
Finally, we’d like to thank all our reviewers and external collaborators:
Kilian Haefeli - Cohere
Benjamin Spector - Flappy Airplanes and Stanford
Neil Movva - Sail Research
Orian Leitersdorf - Decart AI
Hardik Bishnoi - Arcee AI
And many anonymous reviewers
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!eHLl!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ibuq!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
This article is the first in a series exploring low-level assembly and kernel code of AI accelerators. In future installments, we will expand the effort by benc
Join us if you want to work on low level benchmarking, ClusterMAX, inference simulators, or other interesting technical work. Send your resume to letsgo@semiana
From Hopper to Blackwell, NVIDIA made several incremental improvements to the architecture and changes to the PTX abstractions for MMA-related instructions. We
The introduction of tensor memory (TMEM) to hold MMA accumulators. Threads no longer implicitly own the results of MMA operations and instead, TMEM is explicitl
tcgen05 operations are now issued by a single thread on behalf of the entire CTA, rather than at warp or warpgroup scope as in previous generations. You can see
Support for TPC-scoped TMA and MMA across pairs of coordinating CTAs, exposed as cta_group::2 in PTX and 2CTA in SASS, where two SMs making up a TPC can execute
Native support for sub-byte datatypes with micro-scaling
Cluster Launch Control (CLC) as hardware support for dynamic work scheduling in persistent-CTA kernels (Covering in future articles)
Programmatic dependent launch (PDL) was introduced in Hopper to hide launch and setup latency in back-to-back kernels (Covering in future articles)
Since Hopper, Nvidia datacenter GPUs have supported an optional feature which is known by several names, such as “thread block clusters”, “CTA clusters”, and “c
Importantly, CTAs in a cluster are guaranteed to be co-scheduled on the same GPC. This has an important consequence in the 1-CTA-per-SM “persistent CTA”-style B
The number of yielded SMs per GPCs is not fixed, not the same between GPCs on the same chip, and may not even be symmetrical between dies in the same package. M
We prompted Claude to write a utility to reverse-engineer the mapping of SMs to GPCs by launching clusters of various sizes and using PTX %%smid to record which
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VqPc!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
As of SM100, NVIDIA has provided a solution to this quantization issue so that kernels can get the benefit of larger clusters while still making use of all the
References:
Cluster API
Cooperative groups API
CU_LAUNCH_ATTRIBUTE_PREFERRED_CLUSTER_DIMENSION
CUTLASS Example 73
The groupings of TPCs into GPCs we presented above are logical groupings. They represent software’s view of the GPCs, with no information about which of the 20
To discover more information about the physical layout of the SMs, we have every SM traverse a pointer-chase array that fills L2 cache, measuring the latency of
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1JbI!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
We can see two clear sets of SMs, separated by >300 cycles average distance to L2; this must be the die-to-die crossing. We’ve also labeled the SMs with their l
Based on this information, we can refine the list of yielded TPCs for each GPC, though the 5+3 is still just a guess.
Additionally, though in a roundabout way, we can conclude that the die-to-die latency penalty is roughly 300 cycles. This is also evident when looking at the la
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!U0jj!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
We would like to thank Orian from Decart AI for the benchmark inspiration.
In this section, we discuss the memory subsystem: the hardware units that move data between computation units. Memory copy instructions are operations that use
Async copy (PTX: cp.async , SASS: LDGSTS) was introduced in the Ampere generation, and the instruction moves data from global memory to shared memory asynchrono
Referencing FlashInfer Multi-head attention (MHA) kernels, we benchmark async copy with the following configuration:
CTAs per SM: 1, 2, 3, 4
Number of Stages: 1, 2, 4
Threads per CTA: 64, 128, 256
Load Size: 4B, 8B, 16B
We plot throughput versus bytes-in-flight per SM, the total number of bytes concurrent memory loading instructions are loading.
Although different load sizes converge to similar throughput at the same bytes-in-flight, we prefer 16-byte loads. 16-byte loads achieve slightly higher through
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wD4E!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Overall, we see memory throughput with LDGSTS saturating at around 6.6 TB/s at 32 KiB in flight.
We also benchmark config space multi-latent attention (MLA) kernels use:
1 CTA per SM
16B loads
Threads per CTA: 64, 128, 256
Number of Stages: 4, 8, 12, 16
Our experiments show that increasing the number of stages achieves higher throughput at higher bytes-in-flight, and that increasing threads per CTA strictly imp
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Lvbe!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
We benchmarked the latency of the same set of configurations. We see that LDGSTS has a baseline latency of ~600 nanoseconds and nearly doubles after 8 KiB in fl
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!pE_H!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tjkZ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
TMA (PTX: cp.async.bulk.tensor, SASS: UTMALDG) is an asynchronous data copy engine introduced in the Hopper generation, specialized for moving large amounts of
Referencing FlashInfer attention kernels, we benchmark TMA, assigning only one CTA per SM but using one thread for each of 1 to 4 warps per CTA to issue TMA ins
We benchmark TMA with the following configuration:
CTAs per SM: 1
Threads per CTA: 128 (4 warps)
TMA box dimensions: 2D shapes increasing from size 32x8 to 128x128
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!IhCY!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Peak throughput is reached far later than LDGSTS.
Deep learning kernel libraries like FlashInfer use both TMA and async copy for loading data. TMA and async copy have different performance characteristics: TMA
We see that throughput-wise, async copy slightly outperforms TMA at less than 32 bytes in flight, but TMA catches up after that and can continue scaling to 128
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mtqT!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ax25!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
In reality, Blackwell MLA kernels use async copy for dynamically loading pages, while its MHA kernels use only TMA. Most of FlashInfer’s Blackwell MHA kernels a
TMA supports a multicast mode, where a single load copies data to the shared memory of multiple SMs, specified by a CTA mask. Multicast is commonly used in GEMM
According to NCU, the unit responsible for serving TMA multicast requests is called the L2 Request Coalescer (LRC):
The L2 Request Coalescer (LRC) processes incoming requests for L2 and tries to coalesce read requests before forwarding them to the L2 cache. It also serves pro
It sounds like the hardware might provide some multicast behavior, even if it isn’t explicitly requested, like a miss status holding register. We test this by r
Here, we compare three cases:
1. Every SM loads different data (baseline) 2. TMA multicast (explicit) - one CTA in each cluster issues multicast loads to all CTAs in its cluster 3. TMA multi
TMA multicast allows for much higher load bandwidth to fill SMEM buffers, even if data is not already in L2. For known traffic patterns, explicit TMA multicast
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Kl4E!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Implicit multicast performs on par with explicit in terms of effective memory throughput. However, for L2 cache traffic reduction, implicit multicast loses effe
NVIDIA introduced distributed shared memory (DSMEM) in the Hopper architecture. DSMEM allows CTAs within a cluster to access each other’s shared memory. This is
We experimented with a few different PTX patterns for interacting with DSMEM. An important difference when writing code for DSMEM vs. SMEM is that DSMEM loads a
With ld.shared, the compiler emits LDS instead of a generic LD emitted with ld.shared::cluster which does not seem to be able to achieve peak throughput for loc
The peak throughput we achieved when using each PTX pattern is below, expressed as bytes per clock cycle (B/clk) to align with the known max achievable in SM-lo
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!eyO7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
The MMA instruction is the core operation that performs matrix multiplication. MMA performance has grown increasingly shape-dependent from Hopper to Blackwell.
Blackwell comes with 2SM MMA, a new type of MMA instruction (.cta_group::2) where a CTA pair collaboratively executes one MMA operation across 2 SMs. Specifical
We benchmarked MMA performance with a configuration space below:
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Vi8a!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
NVIDIA claims specific throughput performance for different input data types, and here we show their claims for each (format + CTA group) and compare them with
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!gMEj!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
For 1SM MMA across all N sizes, we see that the smaller M=64 achieves max 50% theoretical peak throughput, and the larger M=128 achieves near 100%. This confirm
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7P-g!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
MMA supports two different AB layouts: Both input matrices stored in SMEM (SS), and matrix A stored in TMEM and matrix B stored in SMEM (TS). We observed that f
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!V8NQ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
We can show that this is because the instruction itself is SMEM bandwidth bound below N=128 for SS mode. For example, for FP16 we know the hardware can do 8192
A_bytes = 2*M*K = 4096; B_bytes = 2*N*K = 2048;
FLOPs = 2*M*N*K = 262144
SMEM Cycles = (A_bytes + B_bytes) / (128 B/clk) = 48 cycles
Math Cycles = FLOPs / (16384 FLOPs/clk) = 32 cycles
We compute this for increasing N and find we are finally Math limited starting from the N=128 instruction.
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xHgb!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
The same is true for other datatypes - MMA instructions with both operands in SMEM are SMEM-bound below N=128.
To further illustrate the point, we plot the roofline for all shapes of FP8 1SM MMA. We see clearly that the N < 256 is at the memory-bounded region, and the sl
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-agO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
2SM MMA achieves perfect weak scaling across all formats and shapes, reaching 2x speedup when using 2x the amount of compute resources than 1SM MMA. In smaller
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!pG8O!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
SS mode: Over 2x speedup for N < 128 due to being SMEM bound
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CSsj!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
TS mode: Near-perfect 2x speedup
These experiments show that you should always use the largest instruction shape available for a given SMEM tile size to get maximum throughput.
We benchmarked single MMA instruction latency, and we plot the comparison below. Across all configurations, we see latency linearly increases from N=64 to 128,
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!21tK!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
We notice a small but consistent pattern of the order of latency:
S8 < BF16 = E4M3 = F4 < MXF8 = MXF4
We believe integer operations being more power efficient leads to S8 being the fastest, and scale factor computation introduces a minor overhead for MXF8 and MX
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8pOu!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic

sections.title: Throughput at Different In-Flight Instruction Count

paragraphs.source_preview: In our throughput benchmark, we set high numbers of in-flight instructions to amortize instruction issuing and commit wait overheads, ranging from 256 to 1024.
Across all configurations, we see the same N and in-flight MMAs achieve similar percentages of Speed-of-Light (SoL). Notably, only the largest N reaches 90% SoL
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zi4B!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bQr2!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hvag!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Below we discuss real-world use cases with kernel writing library CUTLASS. We also discuss throughput, multi-cast, and floorplans.
Here we look at a real world use case: the tiled GEMM inner-loop pattern of continuously loading tiles along the inner dimension K to compute a single tile of a
First we use Nvidia’s CUTLASS Library to instantiate a kernel with a single persistent CTA which computes a single output tile. We vary the number of load stage
For a chosen tile size, more and more SMEM can be used to increase the stage count and hide more latency. For a given pipeline depth N, if one stage is actively
However, if the GEMM Epilogue consumes SMEM, there is less available for the main loop and so the stage count used must be lower. Here, we use an epilogue that
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZJVd!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
So SMEM usage and therefore stage count is computed based on:
Operand SMEM tiles (A and B)
Barrier storage
Epilogue SMEM usage
Earlier, we took a look at the achievable throughput of individual instructions; we found that some instructions are likely inherently bound by SMEM bandwidth,
A few such results are shown in the charts, where in each chart we hold the per-CTA tile size constant and vary the cluster size, for both 1SM and 2SM. Note tha
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PF7p!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Blackwell shares a similar floorplan as Blackwell Ultra, shown in the image below. Like we discussed in our previous section, we see 8 GPCs, the L2 cache segmen
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rL3o!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Source: Inside NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra: The Chip Powering the AI Factory Era
We are obtaining Blackwell chip die shots to collect more silicon information, including functional block areas, PHY bandwidth density, detailed floorplan of a
